message.content:
## Synthesis: research report

**Question:** National Life Group (NLG) / Life Insurance Company of the Southwest (LSW) TERM LIFE INSURANCE products — comprehensive product reference. Cover: (1) the full current term lineup and product names (e.g. the "G" guaranteed level-premium series Term 10-G / 15-G / 20-G / 30-G, form series ICC18-20522, and any other current term products like LSW Level Term / annually-renewable term); (2) issue ages and available level term lengths; (3) the level-premium period then annually-renewable-to-age-95 structure, whether the policy has cash value/dividends (it does not); (4) the conversion privilege — window (first 20 years or to age 70 if earlier), no evidence of insurability, equivalent rate class, what permanent products it converts to; (5) the Accelerated Benefit Rider (ABR) living-benefit suite available on term (Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, Alzheimer's) — cost (no additional premium), triggers, discounted/condition-specific payout, and per-insured lifetime benefit limits and how they vary by state; (6) premium structure and rate classes (Elite/Preferred/Select/Standard/Express, tobacco/non-tobacco); (7) state availability variations; (8) foreign-national / non-US-resident rules for term (minimum face, US nexus/solicitation/delivery requirements, whether Brazil/"B" country is eligible, whether term is even offered to foreign nationals). Prioritize NLG/LSW primary sources: nationallife.com, the Life Product Quick Reference Chart, agent guides, product guides, rider brochures, and the Foreign National Guidelines. Distinguish guaranteed/structural facts from rate-sheet-driven current rates (which change often). Produce a cited report with source URLs.

20 claims survived 3-vote adversarial verification. Merge semantic duplicates and synthesize.

## Confirmed claims
### [0] NLG's underwriting guide has a dedicated term underwriting section titled 'Term LSW and Term NL Life,' and the term lineup consists of 10-, 15-, 20-, and 30-Year Term plus ART (Annually Renewable Term), each with distinct issue-age bands (e.g., 10-Year Term issue ages 18-75; 30-Year Term 18-55 nontobacco / 18-50 tobacco; ART 18-85).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: http://www.cassaniinsurance.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/National-Life-Group-Underwriting-Guide-2023.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Life Underwriting Requirements T erm LSW and T erm NL Life"
Verifier evidence (high): Verified against the cited primary source (extracted via pdftotext -layout from the fetched PDF). The guide's table of contents lists a section "Term LSW and Term NL Life .... 15" (line 20), and the section heading appears verbatim at line 770 as "Term LSW and Term NL Life". Immediately below, an "ISSUE AGES OFFERED" block (lines 848-855) states exactly: "ART 18-85 / 10 Year Term 18-75 / 15 Year Term 18-75 Non-tobacco 18-70 Tobacco / 20 Year Term 18-70 Non-tobacco 18-65 Tobacco / 30 Year Term 18-55 Non-tobacco 18-50 Tobacco". A companion product-availability table (line 787) also lists "10 Year Term 18-75". Every specific figure in the claim (section title; the 10/15/20/30-Year Term + ART lineup; 10-Year 18-75; 30-Year 18-55 NT / 18-50 T; ART 18-85) matches the document word-for-word. Issue-age bands are structural underwriting facts (not volatile rates), so 2023 currency is not a disqualifier. Source is a primary agent-facing carrier underwriting guide. No contradicting evidence found; other mirror copies (pinneyinsurance.com, lbfg.net) corroborate. Note: this "Year Term / ART" underwriting framing coexists with the marketing "Level Term xx-G" (form ICC18-20522) naming — same products, different document context — which does not contradict the claim.

### [1] Term products are offered across the Elite / Preferred / Select / Standard / Express rate-class structure split by tobacco use: Elite, Preferred NT, Select NT, Standard NT, Express Standard NT, Preferred Tobacco, Standard Tobacco, and Express Standard Tobacco; Elite has a maximum issue age of 75 while the other classes extend to 85, and Express Standard is limited to total face amounts of $2,000,000 or less.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: http://www.cassaniinsurance.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/National-Life-Group-Underwriting-Guide-2023.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Rate Classes   F ace Amount   Ages Elite ... Preferred NT ... Select NT ... Standard NT ... Express Standard NT ... Preferred T obacco ... Standard T obacco ... Express Standard T obacco"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is fully supported by the primary source itself (the authentic NLG Life Insurance Underwriting Guide, form TC136295(0923)3, page 15, "Term products rate class offerings" table). That table lists exactly: Elite (All Face Amounts, 18-75), Preferred NT (18-85), Select NT (18-85), Standard NT (18-85), Express Standard NT 1 (Total Face ≤ $2,000,000, 18-85), Express Standard NT 2 (Total Face ≤ $2,000,000, 18-85), Preferred Tobacco (18-85), Standard Tobacco (18-85), and Express Standard Tobacco (Total Face ≤ $2,000,000, 18-85). Every element of the claim checks out: (1) the rate-class list matches (the claim collapses "Express Standard NT 1/2" into one "Express Standard NT," a harmless simplification); (2) Elite max issue age = 75 while all other classes extend to 85 — confirmed exactly; (3) all three Express Standard classes are limited to "Total Face ≤ $2,000,000" — confirmed exactly. This is a structural/underwriting fact (not a rate-sheet item subject to frequent change). Source is a genuine NLG primary document, merely mirrored on an agent's website. No contradicting evidence; the source directly and completely substantiates the claim.

### [2] This document is a product of American National Insurance Company, NOT National Life Group / LSW — despite the source list titling it a 'NLG guide, IM10743'. It should not be used as an NLG/LSW primary source.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.cpasy.com/files/82918/im_10743.pdf (primary)
Quote: "American National Insurance Company, Galveston, Texas."
Verifier evidence (high): Direct extraction of the PDF at cpasy.com/files/82918/im_10743.pdf confirms it is an American National Insurance Company document, not NLG/LSW. Title page produced by 'American National Insurance Company'; attribution line 'American National Insurance Company, Galveston, Texas.'; rider forms 'Policy Form Series: ABR14-TM; ABR14-CH; ABR14-CT' (American National's series, not NLG/LSW's ICC18/ICC-series forms). All product examples are American National (IUL, Executive UL, 20-Year Term 'from American National Insurance Company'). A grep of the full 20-page text found ZERO occurrences of 'National Life Group', 'LSW', or 'Life Insurance Company of the Southwest'. The supporting quote appears verbatim. The claim is correct: this is a competitor's ABR guide mislabeled as 'NLG guide IM10743' and must not be used as an NLG/LSW primary source.

### [3] NLG's Accelerated Benefit Rider suite includes coverage for Alzheimer's Disease (and Lewy Body Dementia) as of January 2023, expanding the prior Terminal/Chronic/Critical Illness/Critical Injury set.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.nationallife.com/Our-Story/newsroom/NLG-expand-suite-of-living-benefits-adds-Alzheimers-and-Fertility-Riders (primary)
Quote: "protection if you experience a qualifying terminal, chronic, critical illness, critical injury or a diagnosis of Alzheimer's Disease or Lewy Body Dementia"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is fully supported by the primary source and corroborated by independent trade press. NLG newsroom press release (dated Jan 30, 2023; effective Jan 28, 2023) states the living benefits suite provides protection for "a qualifying terminal, chronic, critical illness, critical injury or a diagnosis of Alzheimer's Disease or Lewy Body Dementia" — precisely the prior four-condition ABR set plus the new Alzheimer's/Lewy Body Dementia addition, matching the claim's wording. The rider is "Available for all life insurance products, depending on state approval," which includes term life (NLG resource-center page confirms availability "with term and permanent life insurance policies"). Independent corroboration: InsuranceNewsNet ("National Life expands living benefits suite") and Wink Intel both reported the same expansion in Feb 2023. Date ("as of January 2023") is accurate. No credible source contradicts or heavily qualifies the claim; the only qualifiers (state approval, no additional premium, specialist diagnosis required) are consistent with, not contradictory to, the claim. Source is a primary manufacturer press release, sufficient for this factual product-feature claim. Minor caveat: it is a company press release (self-reported), but the factual assertion is low-controversy and independently confirmed.

### [4] NLG's Accelerated Benefits Rider pays a benefit calculated as the actuarially discounted value of the death benefit being accelerated, less a pro-rata portion of any policy debt — i.e., the accelerated payout is discounted, not dollar-for-dollar.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.nationallife.com/NWI/Help/en-US/Peach/Riders_ChronicABR2_CriticalABR3.htm (primary)
Quote: "the actuarially discounted value of the death benefit being accelerated less a pro-rata portion of any policy debt."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is verbatim-supported by the primary NLG source (nationallife.com/NWI/Help/en-US/Peach/Riders_ChronicABR2_CriticalABR3.htm), which states the benefit is "the actuarially discounted value of the death benefit being accelerated less a pro-rata portion of any policy debt." WebFetch of the primary page confirmed the payout is "discounted, not paid dollar-for-dollar" via two adjustments (actuarial discounting + pro-rata policy-debt reduction). Corroborated by independent agent/product guides (merrillconnect.iscorp.com NLG doc repository items 103886/68928, cpasy.com im_10743.pdf, theinsgroup.net LSW Living Benefits brochure). The discount reflects mortality/life-expectancy and interest rate — a structural rider feature, not a fast-moving rate-sheet figure, so not outdated. Source is a primary NLG document matching the claim's strength; the paraphrase does not overreach the quote. Minor scope note: the exact quoted page addresses the Chronic/Critical/Critical-Injury ABRs specifically, but the discounted-payment method is NLG's standard ABR structure.

### [5] National Life Group term life policies can be converted to permanent coverage with no underwriting required (no evidence of insurability) as long as the coverage amount is not increased and no additional riders are added.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.nationallife.com/docs/digital/ekit/Term2Perm/index.html (primary)
Quote: "No underwriting required"
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source (nationallife.com Term2Perm ekit) states verbatim: "No underwriting required*" with the asterisk defined as "*Additional underwriting will be required if coverage amount is being increased or additional riders are being added." This is a near-word-for-word match to the claim — no underwriting/no evidence of insurability required PROVIDED the coverage amount is not increased and no additional riders are added. The claim is not an overreach; it faithfully preserves both qualifiers the source attaches to the "no underwriting" statement (rather than stripping the asterisk). Corroborated independently: the LSW Level Term 20-G specimen/product materials describe conversion "without evidence of insurability to any single life permanent plan of life insurance then sold" during the conversion window (first 15 yrs or to age 70, whichever earlier), and the NLG/LSW underwriting guide (pinneyinsurance.com hosted) confirms the term conversion privilege. Source quality is a first-party issuer document appropriate for this (non-extraordinary, standard industry) claim, and it is current. No credible source contradicts or heavily qualifies it beyond the two conditions the claim already includes.

### [6] NLG/LSW's term lineup is the Guaranteed Series of four level-term contracts (G10, G15, G20, G30) with current premiums guaranteed level for 10, 15, 20, or 30 years respectively, plus a separate Annual Renewable Term (ART) product with premiums that increase annually.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.fmiagent.com/wp-content/uploads/2025-11-12_National_Life_Group_Life_Product_Quick_Reference_Chart_63236_10-25.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Guaranteed Series provides the longest guaranteed premium period through four level term life insurance contracts — G10, G15, G20, G30. Each contract is competitively priced with current premiums guaranteed to remain level for 10, 15, 20, or 30 years, respectively. Annual Renewable Term (ART) is a competitively priced, annually renewable term life insurance policy with guaranteed premiums that increase annually."
Verifier evidence (high): Primary source verified verbatim. The NLG Life Product Quick Reference Chart (item 63236, dated 10-25), extracted via pdftotext from the fmiagent.com PDF, states in the Term row: "Guaranteed Series provides the longest guaranteed premium period through four level term life insurance contracts -- G10, G15, G20, G30." / "Each contract is competitively priced with current premiums guaranteed to remain level for 10, 15, 20, or 30 years, respectively." / "Annual Renewable Term (ART) is a competitively priced, annually renewable term life insurance policy with guaranteed premiums that increase annually." The claim reproduces this without overreach. Corroborated by NLG's Life Product Guide (merrillconnect.iscorp.com item 63236) and the LSW Level Term 20-G specimen (level premiums first 20 yrs, then annually renewable to age 95). No contradicting source found. Source is NLG's own current (Oct 2025) agent product chart — primary and appropriate for the claim's strength. These are structural product facts, not a cherry-picked marketing benchmark.

### [7] The term products provide a level amount of coverage during the term period with no cash value accumulation, and offer the lowest initial cost of NLG's product lineup.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.fmiagent.com/wp-content/uploads/2025-11-12_National_Life_Group_Life_Product_Quick_Reference_Chart_63236_10-25.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Term offers the lowest initial cost of all our products and a level amount of life insurance coverage during the term period with no cash value accumulation."
Verifier evidence (high): Verified verbatim in the primary source PDF (NLG Life Product Quick Reference Chart, form 63236 rev 10-25, saved locally, extracted line 283): "Term offers the lowest initial cost of all our products and a level amount of life insurance coverage during the term period with no cash value accumulation." The claim is a faithful paraphrase, not an overreach. "No cash value" is a structural fact of term insurance, corroborated by the same chart contrasting term against WL/IUL products that cite guaranteed cash-value growth (1.75-2.00%). "Lowest initial cost of all our products" is an intra-lineup comparison (term vs. NLG's permanent products) that is definitionally true. Source is primary (NLG's own current chart, dated 10/2025, current as of 2026-07). No contradicting evidence found; facts are structural not rate-driven.

### [8] NLG/LSW term includes an Accelerated Benefit Rider living-benefit suite covering Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, Alzheimer's Disease and Lewy Body Dementia, plus a Children's Term rider, and conversion privileges (with the Waiver of Premium Rider waiving premium on the converted policy).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.fmiagent.com/wp-content/uploads/2025-11-12_National_Life_Group_Life_Product_Quick_Reference_Chart_63236_10-25.pdf (primary)
Quote: "ABR Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, Alzheimer's Disease and Lewy Body Dementia, Children's Term. Competitive Premiums. Conversion Privileges. Waiver of Premium Rider waives premium on converted policy."
Verifier evidence (high): Every component of the claim is independently corroborated by NLG/LSW primary and distributor sources. (1) ABR suite Terminal/Chronic/Critical Illness/Critical Injury: NLG's ABR Agent Guide (theinsgroup.net LSW_Living_Benefits_Rider.pdf) and merrillconnect.iscorp.com/nlg confirm "ABR for Terminal Illness, ABR for Chronic Illness, ABR for Critical Illness and Critical Injury," and note NLG is "the only major carrier that includes critical injury as a standard rider on term." (2) Alzheimer's + Lewy Body Dementia: nationallife.com resource center and the newsroom release "NLG Expand Suite of Living Benefits Adds Alzheimer's" confirm the Alzheimer's Disease Rider "may provide accelerated benefits when the insured has a qualifying diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease or Lewy Body Dementia," available on term and permanent policies. (3) Children's Term rider + Waiver of Premium on term: distributor sourcing quotes NLG language verbatim: "All term plans offer optional Accelerated Benefits Riders, as well as the Children's Term Rider and the Waiver of Premium Rider." (4) Conversion privileges are a standard structural feature of NLG term. The supporting quote is a faithful transcription of the 10/25 Quick Reference Chart, and the only element I could not pin to a separate corroborating source verbatim — WP "waives premium on converted policy" — is a standard, plausible WP provision stated directly in the quoted chart. No source contradicts or qualifies the claim. Note: PDF text-extraction of the two primary PDFs failed (image/binary encoding), so corroboration rests on NLG's own web pages/newsroom plus distributor restatements rather than direct read of the cited chart, but these fully align.

### [9] NLG/LSW offer three Accelerated Benefits Riders — Terminal Illness, Chronic Illness, and Critical Illness — at no additional premium cost; they can be added to most NLIC and LSW life policies. Notably, this guide lists NO separate Critical Injury or Alzheimer's riders (those are later additions to the suite).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://theinsgroup.net/images/stories/LSW/Life/LSW_Living_Benefits_Rider.pdf (primary)
Quote: "The Accelerated Benefits Riders are no additional cost riders that can be added to most National Life Insurance Company (NLIC) and Life Insurance Company of the Southwest (LSW) life insurance policies to help protect the insured from the potential financial devastation of a Terminal, Chronic or Critical Illness."
Verifier evidence (high): Extracted the full text of the cited primary source (NLG/LSW agent guide, form 68928 / MK10775(0514) / TC79353(0514), dated May 2014). Its "Accelerated Benefits Riders" list contains exactly three riders: Terminal Illness, Chronic Illness, Critical Illness — and the ABR Quick Reference and table of contents contain NO Critical Injury or Alzheimer's rider. The Quick Reference explicitly states "Additional Premium Required: No.", and the introduction confirms the riders attach to "National Life Insurance Company and Life Insurance Company of the Southwest insurance policies." NLG's own newsroom (nationallife.com) confirms the Alzheimer's rider was added Jan 28, 2023 — a later addition, consistent with the claim. Current NLG materials show Critical Injury now in the suite but it is absent from this 2014 guide. Every element of the claim (three riders, no additional cost, NLIC+LSW policies, this guide omits Critical Injury/Alzheimer's as later additions) is directly supported by the primary document. Claim is carefully scoped to "this guide lists," which matches the source exactly.

### [10] The per-insured maximum lifetime accelerated benefit limits are Terminal $1,500,000, Chronic $1,500,000 (Chronic for NY $2,000,000), and Critical $1,000,000, applied across all contracts on a single insured, and limits vary by state (e.g., benefit limited to $500,000 in IL and NJ for Terminal, Chronic, and Critical).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://theinsgroup.net/images/stories/LSW/Life/LSW_Living_Benefits_Rider.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Chronic For NY Only: $2,000,000 / Terminal: $1,500,000 / Chronic: $1,500,000 / Critical: $1,000,000"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim fully supported by primary source (LSW Accelerated Benefits Riders Agent Guide) and independent corroboration. The guide states National Life "limits the amount of death benefit that may be accelerated under all contracts made over the entire lifetime of the insured to $1,500,000 for terminal or chronic illness, and $1,000,000 for critical illness or injury" — matching Terminal $1,500,000, Chronic $1,500,000, Critical $1,000,000, applied across all contracts on a single insured. The Chronic-for-NY $2,000,000 figure appears verbatim in the source quote. The $500,000 IL/NJ limit is independently confirmed ("The benefit is limited to $500,000 in IL and NJ"). Source is a primary carrier product/agent document, and these are structural per-insured lifetime maximums (not volatile rate-sheet data), so source quality matches claim strength. No contradicting evidence found.

### [11] LSW's guaranteed level-premium term series in this guide comprises 10-G, 15-G/NG, 20-G/NG, and 30-G/NG, with maximum issue ages that shorten as the term lengthens: 10-G issues to age 75, 15-G/NG to age 75, 20-G/NG to age 70, and 30-G/NG to age 55 (all from issue age 18).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.totalfinancial.com/carriers/downloads/lsw/lsw-uw1.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Ages 18-75: 10-G: All Face Amounts ... Ages 18-75: 15-G/NG: All Face Amounts ... Ages 18-70: 20-G/NG: All Face Amounts ... Ages 18-55: 30G/NG: All Face Amounts"
Verifier evidence (high): The supporting quote is verbatim-accurate to the primary source. Extracting text from the LSW Life Insurance Underwriting Guide PDF (totalfinancial.com/carriers/downloads/lsw/lsw-uw1.pdf), the "Product / Issue Age" table reads exactly: "Ages 18-75: 10-G: All Face Amounts / Ages 18-75: 15-G/NG: All Face Amounts / Ages 18-70: 20-G/NG: All Face Amounts / Ages 18-55: 30G/NG: All Face Amounts." This directly supports every enumerated figure in the claim (10-G to 75, 15-G/NG to 75, 20-G/NG to 70, 30-G/NG to 55, all from 18). I independently corroborated with the official LSW Level Term Product Guide (theinsgroup.net/images/stories/LSW/Life/LSW_Level_Term_Product_Guide.pdf), whose per-product "Issue ages (age nearest birthday)" sections show: 10-G = "18 - 75"; 15-G/NG = "18 - 75 Non-tobacco"; 20-G/NG = "18 - 70 Non-tobacco"; 30-G/NG = "18 - 55 Non-tobacco." Two independent sources agree on the exact figures. Minor nuance (not refuting): the phrase "shorten as the term lengthens" is loosely worded since 10-G and 15-G share the same max age of 75 (non-strictly-decreasing), and the product guide reveals tobacco classes have slightly lower caps (15/20/30 tobacco = 70/65/50); but the specific load-bearing max-age numbers the claim states are all correct. Source quality (carrier product guide + underwriting guide) matches the claim's strength; these are structural product facts, not fast-moving rate-sheet data.

### [12] LSW/National Life does NOT offer term insurance to foreign nationals; only permanent plans are eligible for foreign-national applicants, and eligibility is limited to citizens/residents of 'A' or 'B' classified countries.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.totalfinancial.com/carriers/downloads/lsw/lsw-uw1.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Only permanent plans of insurance will be considered. No term insurance is eligible. ... Only citizens / residents of countries classiﬁed as “A” or “B” countries will be considered."
Verifier evidence (high): CONFIRMED by current primary source. The official National Life Group "Foreign National Guidelines" (Cat No 69798(1125), TC8575778(1125)3, current 11/2025 catalog revision), hosted on nationallife.com and mirrored at fmiagent.com, states in its Product table (p.4, Life column) verbatim: "Permanent Products Only — up to Class 4 (200%)". This directly confirms term is not offered to foreign nationals. The same table specifies "A & B countries only" for face amounts and "ABRs Only — A & B countries only" for riders; "In D & E countries, we cannot offer coverage"; C countries are facultative/individual consideration. Brazil is listed as a B country (p.7), so it is eligible for permanent (not term). The original quote from totalfinancial.com/lsw-uw1.pdf ("Only permanent plans of insurance will be considered. No term insurance is eligible...Only citizens/residents of countries classified as A or B") could not be text-extracted (compressed scan) but is corroborated in substance by this current, higher-quality primary source. Minor over-precision: C countries get individual facultative consideration, so "limited to A or B" is the standard/retention rule rather than absolute, but this does not undercut the core claim. Claim is accurate, current, and backed by a carrier primary source.

### [13] LSW's Accelerated Benefit Rider suite in this brochure comprises three riders — ABR1 (Terminal Illness), ABR2 (Chronic Illness), and ABR3 (Critical Illness) — each available at no additional premium.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.marketingresources.us.com/core/lsw/LBrider.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Accelerated Benefits Rider1 (Terminal Illness) ... Accelerated Benefits Rider2 (Chronic Illness) ... Accelerated Benefits Rider3 (Critical Illness) ... All riders except for the Accelerated Benefits and Unemployment Riders require an additional premium."
Verifier evidence (high): Extracted the primary PDF (via pdftotext) and confirmed every element verbatim. The rider portfolio lists exactly three ABRs: "Accelerated Benefits Rider1 (Terminal Illness)", "Accelerated Benefits Rider2 (Chronic Illness)", "Accelerated Benefits Rider3 (Critical Illness)". Each detail section states it plainly: "ABR1 is available at no additional premium," "ABR2 is available at no additional premium," "ABR3 is available at no additional premium." The footnote confirms: "All riders except for the Accelerated Benefits and Unemployment Riders require an additional premium." The claim is precisely scoped to "in this brochure" and is fully accurate for it. CAVEAT (does not refute the claim as worded): this is a legacy ~2010 brochure (form 62824 MK2333(0610); cites 2007-08 stats; ABR form series 8052(0798)/8095(0399)/8165(0703) on LSW Harbor/Horizon UL). The CURRENT NLG term ABR suite (ICC18 series) expands beyond three to add Critical Injury (and Alzheimer's variants). So this 3-rider set must NOT be presented as the current offering — but the claim's "in this brochure" scoping keeps it accurate.

### [14] The ABR benefit is paid on a discounted basis (payment less than face accelerated), and for ABR3 the level of discounting varies by the insured's age and the severity of the critical illness.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.marketingresources.us.com/core/lsw/LBrider.pdf (primary)
Quote: "With ABR3, the level of discounting applied to the death benefit varies depending upon the insured's age and the severity of the critical illness. ... the actual ABR payment you receive will be less than the death benefit you accelerate."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is verbatim-supported by the cited primary source (marketingresources.us.com/core/lsw/LBrider.pdf, "Living Benefits: A Key to Life Insurance," TC55294/MK2333), which states: "With ABR3, the level of discounting applied to the death benefit varies depending upon the insured's age and the severity of the critical illness. If you choose to use your death benefit early, the actual ABR payment you receive will be less than the death benefit you accelerate." This is corroborated by independent NLG/LSW primary sources: the LSW ABR Agent Guide (theinsgroup.net/.../LSW_Living_Benefits_Rider.pdf) and NLG's own "Accelerated Benefit Riders: Your Guide to Living Benefits" (im_10743.pdf), which explain the payment is the actuarially discounted value of the accelerated death benefit less future costs, with the discount driven by the insured's life expectancy at election. This is a structural/guaranteed feature of the rider (not a rate-sheet current-rate item), so it is not subject to the outdating concern. No credible source contradicts it; the "discounted-basis" mechanism is standard for accelerated benefit riders across the industry. Attempted refutation on all three axes (quote overreach, contradiction, source quality) failed: quote matches the claim exactly, no contradicting evidence exists, and the source is a first-party NLG/LSW marketing/product document appropriate for this structural product fact. Only minor caveat: the exact discount factors and the $500 max administrative fee are rate/admin details that can change, but the qualitative claim (discounted payout; ABR3 discount varies by age and illness severity) is well-established.

### [15] For foreign nationals, National Life Group / LSW offers only permanent life insurance products — term life is NOT available to foreign nationals (the Product section restricts life sales to 'Permanent Products Only').
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.fmiagent.com/wp-content/uploads/2025-11-12_National_Life_Group_Foreign_National_Guidelines_69798_11-25.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Permanent Products Only — up to Class 4 (200%)"
Verifier evidence (high): CONFIRMED by primary source. The NLG Foreign National Guidelines PDF (Cat No 69798(1125), dated 11/2025), page 4 "Product" section, "Life" column states verbatim: "Permanent Products Only — up to Class 4 (200%)". Immediately below it lists "RapidProtect/RapidProtect NL not available for Foreign Nationals" — RapidProtect is NLG/LSW's term/accelerated-underwriting product line, explicitly excluded. The claim that term life is NOT available to foreign nationals is directly and precisely supported by the exact quote, not an overreach. Corroboration: independent WebSearch result stated "To obtain coverage with National Life Group, it must be for permanent life insurance only, as the company does not offer term life insurance" for foreign nationals. Source is a primary NLG agent guide, current (11/2025), and matches claim strength. No contradicting evidence found. Note: this restriction is product-availability for foreign nationals only; NLG/LSW does offer term (e.g., the G-series) to U.S. residents — but that does not affect this specific claim.

### [16] For foreign nationals, National Life Group offers PERMANENT products ONLY (up to Class 4 / 200% table rating) — term life insurance is not available to foreign nationals.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://nfgbrokerage.com/wp-content/uploads/National-Life-Foreign-National-Guidelines.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Permanent Products Only – up to Class 4 (200%)"
Verifier evidence (high): Primary source (National-Life-Foreign-National-Guidelines.pdf) text extraction directly confirms the quote: "Permanent Products Only – up to Class 4 (200%)" appears in the Foreign National Rules section. This structurally means only permanent products are offered to foreign nationals — the "Class 4 (200%)" refers to the maximum table rating on those permanent products, not a term option. Independent corroboration: RiskQuoter.com states "To obtain coverage with National Life Group, it must be for permanent life insurance only, as the company does not offer term life insurance." A current 2025-11-12 version of the same NLG guidelines exists (fmiagent.com), confirming the rule is not outdated. The claim is a faithful reading of the quote, not an overreach; source is primary and current.

### [17] The minimum face amount for foreign national life insurance is $500,000, with a maximum of $15,000,000 ($2,000,000 for students).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://nfgbrokerage.com/wp-content/uploads/National-Life-Foreign-National-Guidelines.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Face amount minimum: $500,000 ... Face amount maximum: $15,000,000 (for students the maximum is $2,000,000)"
Verifier evidence (high): Direct text extraction (pdftotext) of the primary source PDF (nfgbrokerage.com/wp-content/uploads/National-Life-Foreign-National-Guidelines.pdf) reproduces the claim verbatim: "Face amount minimum: $500,000 / Face amount maximum: $15,000,000 / (for students the maximum is $2,000,000)". This is corroborated by the current 2025 mirror of the same NLG document (fmiagent.com .../National_Life_Group_Foreign_National_Guidelines_69798_11-25.pdf). The $500,000 face-amount minimum is distinct from a separate $500,000 US-assets requirement ("significant documented assets in the U.S. (minimum $500,000)... for at least 3 months"). Country-level max faces also confirm A/B countries = $15,000,000 max. The claim is quoted accurately, current, and from a primary NLG source. Sole caveat (not a refutation): these amounts apply to "Permanent Products Only – up to Class 4 (200%)", since term is not offered to foreign nationals; the claim's phrasing "foreign national life insurance" remains accurate.

### [18] Foreign national policies require U.S. nexus, U.S. delivery to a valid address in the state of issue, all solicitation/sales in the U.S., and English-language application/policy forms; a foreign national is anyone spending more than 4 months outside the U.S. in a consecutive 12-month period.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://nfgbrokerage.com/wp-content/uploads/National-Life-Foreign-National-Guidelines.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Policy must be delivered in U.S. to a valid address in the state of issue ... All solicitation and sales activities must take place in the U.S.; no marketing or policy materials may be transmitted or delivered outside the U.S. ... All application and policy forms must be in English"
Verifier evidence (high): All five clauses are quoted verbatim from the primary NLG Foreign National Guidelines PDF (page 3). Definition: "National Life Group considers 'ANY' individual who spends more than 4 months (in a consecutive 12 month period) outside the U.S. a foreign national... This includes U.S. citizens living abroad." Delivery: "Policy must be delivered in U.S. to a valid address in the state of issue." Solicitation: "All solicitation and sales activities must take place in the U.S.; no marketing or policy materials may be transmitted or delivered outside the U.S." English forms: "All application and policy forms must be in English." Nexus: "Foreign national insureds and owners... must have verifiable proof of one or more of the following connections/nexus to the United States" plus "National Life will not accept foreign nationals that do not have a nexus to the U.S." Source is the primary agent guideline itself, matching claim strength. Minor caveat (non-refuting): doc dated May 2017 (TC 0221), and it restricts FN eligibility to permanent products only — but neither point contradicts the definition/requirements claim under review.

### [19] LSW's level-term lineup in this guide consists of seven level-premium, level-benefit products — fully guaranteed 10-G, 15-G, 20-G, 30-G plus 15-NG, 20-NG, 30-NG (guaranteed 10 years, current-level thereafter) — with coverage on all plans extending to age 95.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: http://theinsgroup.net/images/stories/LSW/Life/LSW_Level_Term_Product_Guide.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Life Insurance Company of the Southwest is pleased to bring you seven level premium, level benefit term life products... We have fully guaranteed level premium plans of 10, 15, 20 and 30 years, as well as 15, 20, and 30-year level premium plans with a 10-year guarantee."
Verifier evidence (high): Verified by extracting the primary-source PDF text (pdftotext). Intro reads verbatim: "seven level premium, level benefit term life products... fully guaranteed level premium plans of 10, 15, 20 and 30 years, as well as 15, 20, and 30-year level premium plans with a 10-year guarantee." The document uses the exact product labels 10-G, 15-G, 20-G, 30-G and 15-NG, 20-NG, 30-NG ("LSW Level Term 10-G"; "LSW Level Term 15-G and LSW Level Term 15-NG"; etc.). The -NG plans are described as "current level premiums for [N] years; guaranteed level for the first 10." "Coverage under [this/both] plan[s] extends to age 95" appears on all four product pages (10/15/20/30), with "policy remains in force until the policy anniversary following the insured's 95th birthday." Every clause of the claim is directly supported. Sole caveat: guide is form MK2303(0913), dated 2013, but the claim is explicitly scoped "in this guide," so it is not an overreach.


## Refuted claims (for transparency)
- "NLG/LSW's current term lineup is branded 'Term LSW and Term NL Life' and offers level-term lengths of ART (annually renewable term), 10-year, 15-year, 20-year, and 30-year." (https://pinneyinsurance.com/underwriting-docs/National%20Life%20Group-LSW%20Underwriting_Guide.pdf, vote 1-2)
- "Term rate classes are Elite, Preferred NT, Standard NT, Express Standard NT, Preferred Tobacco, Standard Tobacco, and Express Standard Tobacco, with maximum issue ages that shrink as the level-term period lengthens (e.g., Elite is 18-85 for ART/10-yr but 18-55 for 30-yr)." (https://pinneyinsurance.com/underwriting-docs/National%20Life%20Group-LSW%20Underwriting_Guide.pdf, vote 1-2)
- "ABRs are available on the term products: the availability grid shows NL ART, NL L Series, NL GL Series, and LSW Term all offer Terminal, Chronic, and Critical ABRs; NL L Series and NL GL Series also offer Chronic for NY, while LSW Term and NL ART do not, and NL ART does not offer Critical." (https://theinsgroup.net/images/stories/LSW/Life/LSW_Living_Benefits_Rider.pdf, vote 1-2)
- "LSW limits the total death benefit that may be accelerated under all accelerated benefit riders applying to the same insured to $1,000,000, and reserves the right to change the limit but never below $500,000." (https://www.marketingresources.us.com/core/lsw/LBrider.pdf, vote 1-2)
- "The Chronic and Critical Illness accelerated benefit riders are not available in all states — ABR2 (Chronic) is unavailable in CT, KS, LA, MN, MT, ND, OR, WA and ABR3 (Critical) is unavailable in CT, MA, MT, NJ, ND." (https://www.marketingresources.us.com/core/lsw/LBrider.pdf, vote 0-3)

## Instructions
1. Identify claims that say the same thing — merge them, combine their sources.
2. Group related claims into coherent findings. Each finding should directly address the research question.
3. Assign confidence per finding: high (multiple primary sources, unanimous votes), medium (secondary sources or split votes), low (single source or blog-quality).
4. Write a 3-5 sentence executive summary answering the research question.
5. Note caveats: what's uncertain, what sources were weak, what time-sensitivity applies.
6. List 2-4 open questions that emerged but weren't answered.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use when: morning brief, daily brief, set up my morning brief, what's my day look like, run my brief.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll synthesize the verified claims into a structured research report.

input.summary: National Life Group (NLG) and its subsidiary Life Insurance Company of the Southwest (LSW) market term life insurance as the "Guaranteed Series" — four fully-guaranteed level-premium contracts (G10, G15, G20, G30, current form era ICC18-20522) plus an Annual Renewable Term (ART) product; older LSW materials also expose 15-NG/20-NG/30-NG variants (10-year guarantee, current-level thereafter) and label the section "Term LSW and Term NL Life." Term is level-benefit with no cash value and the lowest initial cost in the lineup; issue ages run 18 to 75 for shorter terms and shorten as term lengthens (30-year to 18-55 NT / 18-50 tobacco; ART to 18-85), with coverage on all plans extending to age 95. Rate classes are Elite (max age 75), Preferred/Select/Standard/Express NT and Preferred/Standard/Express tobacco (to age 85, Express capped at $2M face), and policies convert to permanent coverage with no evidence of insurability provided face is not increased and no riders are added. All term plans carry a no-additional-cost Accelerated Benefit Rider suite — Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, and (added Jan 2023) Alzheimer's/Lewy Body Dementia — paying an actuarially discounted (not dollar-for-dollar) benefit, subject to per-insured lifetime maximums (Terminal $1.5M, Chronic $1.5M / $2M NY, Critical $1M, reduced to $500K in IL/NJ). Critically, term is NOT available to foreign nationals — NLG/LSW offers permanent products only to non-US-residents (A/B countries, $500K minimum / $15M maximum face), so Brazil ("B" country) applicants cannot buy term.

findings.claim: The current NLG/LSW term lineup is the 'Guaranteed Series' — four fully-guaranteed level-premium contracts branded G10, G15, G20, G30 (level premiums guaranteed for 10/15/20/30 years respectively) — plus a separate Annual Renewable Term (ART) product with premiums that increase annually. Older LSW product guides additionally expose 15-NG/20-NG/30-NG variants (current-level premiums with only a 10-year guarantee). The current marketing form series is ICC18-20522 ('Level Term xx-G'); the same products appear under the underwriting-document framing 'Term LSW and Term NL Life.'
findings.evidence: The NLG Life Product Quick Reference Chart (item 63236, rev 10-25) states verbatim that the 'Guaranteed Series provides the longest guaranteed premium period through four level term life insurance contracts -- G10, G15, G20, G30' with premiums 'guaranteed to remain level for 10, 15, 20, or 30 years, respectively,' and describes ART as 'annually renewable term life insurance policy with guaranteed premiums that increase annually.' The 2013 LSW Level Term Product Guide describes 'seven level premium, level benefit term life products' — fully guaranteed 10/15/20/30-G plus 15/20/30-year plans with a 10-year guarantee (15-NG/20-NG/30-NG). The 2023 NLG Underwriting Guide contains a section 'Term LSW and Term NL Life' covering the 10/15/20/30-Year Term + ART lineup — the same products under a different document framing.

findings.claim: Term products provide a level amount of coverage during the term period with NO cash value accumulation or dividends, and offer the lowest initial cost of all NLG products. The level-premium period is followed by an annually-renewable structure with coverage extending to the policy anniversary following the insured's 95th birthday.
findings.evidence: The NLG Life Product Quick Reference Chart (form 63236, rev 10-25) states verbatim: 'Term offers the lowest initial cost of all our products and a level amount of life insurance coverage during the term period with no cash value accumulation.' The 2013 LSW Level Term Product Guide confirms on all four product pages that 'coverage under [this] plan extends to age 95' and 'the policy remains in force until the policy anniversary following the insured's 95th birthday.' No cash value is a structural feature of term, corroborated by the chart contrasting term against WL/IUL products that cite guaranteed cash-value growth.

findings.claim: Issue ages for level-term products start at age 18 and the maximum issue age shortens as the term lengthens: 10-G to age 75; 15-G/NG to 75 (18-70 tobacco); 20-G/NG to 70 (18-65 tobacco); 30-G/NG to 55 non-tobacco / 50 tobacco; ART issues 18-85.
findings.evidence: The LSW Life Insurance Underwriting Guide product/issue-age table reads: 'Ages 18-75: 10-G ... Ages 18-75: 15-G/NG ... Ages 18-70: 20-G/NG ... Ages 18-55: 30G/NG.' The 2023 NLG Underwriting Guide 'ISSUE AGES OFFERED' block states: 'ART 18-85 / 10 Year Term 18-75 / 15 Year Term 18-75 Non-tobacco 18-70 Tobacco / 20 Year Term 18-70 Non-tobacco 18-65 Tobacco / 30 Year Term 18-55 Non-tobacco 18-50 Tobacco.' Two independent primary sources agree on the load-bearing figures.

findings.claim: Term is offered across an Elite / Preferred / Select / Standard / Express rate-class structure split by tobacco use (Elite, Preferred NT, Select NT, Standard NT, Express Standard NT, Preferred Tobacco, Standard Tobacco, Express Standard Tobacco). Elite has a maximum issue age of 75; all other classes extend to 85. Express Standard classes are limited to total face amounts of $2,000,000 or less.
findings.evidence: The 2023 NLG Underwriting Guide 'Term products rate class offerings' table lists exactly: Elite (All Face Amounts, 18-75), Preferred NT (18-85), Select NT (18-85), Standard NT (18-85), Express Standard NT 1 and 2 (Total Face <= $2,000,000, 18-85), Preferred Tobacco (18-85), Standard Tobacco (18-85), Express Standard Tobacco (Total Face <= $2,000,000, 18-85). Elite max age 75 vs 85 for others, and the $2M Express cap, are confirmed exactly. These are structural underwriting facts, not volatile rate-sheet figures.

findings.claim: NLG term policies convert to permanent coverage with no underwriting / no evidence of insurability required, provided the coverage amount is not increased and no additional riders are added. Conversion is to a single-life permanent plan then offered by the company, during a conversion window (e.g. first 15-20 years or to age 70, whichever is earlier).
findings.evidence: The NLG Term2Perm ekit states verbatim: 'No underwriting required*' with the asterisk defined as '*Additional underwriting will be required if coverage amount is being increased or additional riders are being added.' Corroborated by the LSW Level Term 20-G specimen/product materials describing conversion 'without evidence of insurability to any single life permanent plan of life insurance then sold' during the conversion window (first 15 yrs or to age 70, whichever earlier).

findings.claim: All NLG/LSW term plans include an Accelerated Benefit Rider (living-benefits) suite at NO additional premium cost. As of January 2023 the suite covers Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, and Alzheimer's Disease / Lewy Body Dementia (the Alzheimer's/Lewy Body addition expanded the prior four-condition set effective Jan 28, 2023). NLG markets itself as the only major carrier including Critical Injury as a standard rider on term. A Children's Term rider and Waiver of Premium rider (which waives premium on the converted policy) are also available.
findings.evidence: The NLG newsroom press release (Jan 30, 2023, effective Jan 28, 2023) states the suite provides protection for 'a qualifying terminal, chronic, critical illness, critical injury or a diagnosis of Alzheimer's Disease or Lewy Body Dementia,' available on all life products subject to state approval. The NLG Life Product Quick Reference Chart (10-25) lists in the term row: 'ABR Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, Alzheimer's Disease and Lewy Body Dementia, Children's Term ... Conversion Privileges. Waiver of Premium Rider waives premium on converted policy.' The 2014 LSW Living Benefits guide confirms ABRs are 'no additional cost riders.' Corroborated by InsuranceNewsNet and Wink Intel trade press.

findings.claim: The Accelerated Benefit Rider pays a discounted benefit — the actuarially discounted value of the death benefit being accelerated, less a pro-rata portion of any policy debt — NOT a dollar-for-dollar acceleration. The degree of discounting reflects the insured's life expectancy/age and, for the Critical Illness rider (ABR3), the severity of the qualifying condition; a per-election administrative fee (historically up to $500) may apply.
findings.evidence: The NLG rider help page states the benefit is 'the actuarially discounted value of the death benefit being accelerated less a pro-rata portion of any policy debt.' The LSW 'Living Benefits' brochure states 'With ABR3, the level of discounting applied to the death benefit varies depending upon the insured's age and the severity of the critical illness' and 'the actual ABR payment you receive will be less than the death benefit you accelerate.' Multiple independent NLG/LSW product guides corroborate the discounted-value mechanism.

findings.claim: Per-insured maximum lifetime accelerated-benefit limits apply across all contracts on a single insured: Terminal $1,500,000; Chronic $1,500,000 ($2,000,000 in NY); Critical Illness/Injury $1,000,000. These limits vary by state — notably reduced to $500,000 in Illinois and New Jersey for terminal, chronic, and critical benefits.
findings.evidence: The LSW Accelerated Benefits Riders Agent Guide states National Life 'limits the amount of death benefit that may be accelerated under all contracts made over the entire lifetime of the insured to $1,500,000 for terminal or chronic illness, and $1,000,000 for critical illness or injury,' with 'Chronic For NY Only: $2,000,000.' The $500,000 IL/NJ limit is independently confirmed in the same guide.

findings.claim: NLG/LSW does NOT offer term insurance to foreign nationals / non-US-residents — only permanent life insurance products are eligible (up to Class 4 / 200% table rating). The RapidProtect/RapidProtect NL term line is explicitly excluded for foreign nationals. Eligibility is limited to citizens/residents of 'A' or 'B' classified countries (Brazil is a 'B' country, so eligible for permanent but not term; C countries get individual facultative consideration; D and E countries cannot be covered).
findings.evidence: The current NLG Foreign National Guidelines (Cat No 69798, rev 11-25), page 4 Product/Life column, states verbatim: 'Permanent Products Only -- up to Class 4 (200%)' and 'RapidProtect/RapidProtect NL not available for Foreign Nationals.' Riders are 'ABRs Only -- A & B countries only'; 'In D & E countries, we cannot offer coverage.' Brazil is listed as a B country (p.7). The older totalfinancial.com LSW underwriting guide states 'Only permanent plans of insurance will be considered. No term insurance is eligible... Only citizens/residents of countries classified as A or B.' Independent web sources confirm NLG offers permanent-only to foreign nationals.

findings.claim: Foreign-national life policies (permanent only) carry a minimum face amount of $500,000 and a maximum of $15,000,000 ($2,000,000 for students). They require a documented US nexus, US delivery to a valid address in the state of issue, all solicitation/sales activity conducted within the US (no marketing or policy materials transmitted outside the US), and English-language application and policy forms. NLG defines a foreign national as anyone (including US citizens living abroad) spending more than 4 months outside the US in a consecutive 12-month period, and requires significant documented US assets (minimum $500,000).
findings.evidence: The NLG Foreign National Guidelines PDF states verbatim: 'Face amount minimum: $500,000 / Face amount maximum: $15,000,000 (for students the maximum is $2,000,000)'; 'Policy must be delivered in U.S. to a valid address in the state of issue'; 'All solicitation and sales activities must take place in the U.S.; no marketing or policy materials may be transmitted or delivered outside the U.S.'; 'All application and policy forms must be in English'; and defines a foreign national as anyone spending 'more than 4 months (in a consecutive 12 month period) outside the U.S.' These face amounts apply to permanent products since term is not offered to foreign nationals.

findings.claim: CAUTION: The document circulated as 'NLG guide IM10743' (cpasy.com/files/82918/im_10743.pdf) is actually an American National Insurance Company document, NOT an NLG/LSW source. It should be excluded from any NLG/LSW term reference.
findings.evidence: Text extraction of the PDF confirms it is produced by 'American National Insurance Company, Galveston, Texas' with rider forms in American National's ABR14-TM/ABR14-CH/ABR14-CT series (not NLG/LSW's ICC18 forms). A full-text search found zero occurrences of 'National Life Group', 'LSW', or 'Life Insurance Company of the Southwest.' All product examples are American National products.

input.caveats: Time-sensitivity: Actual premium dollar amounts are rate-sheet-driven and change frequently — this report deliberately covers only structural/guaranteed facts (product names, issue ages, structure, rate-class labels, conversion terms, rider triggers and lifetime limits), not current rates. Document currency varies: the strongest structural sources range from the 2013 LSW Level Term Product Guide (form MK2303) and 2014 LSW Living Benefits guide (which predate the Critical Injury and Alzheimer's rider additions) through the 2023 NLG Underwriting Guide to the current Oct/Nov 2025 Quick Reference Chart and Foreign National Guidelines (69798, 11-25). Where a fact is scoped to a specific older document (e.g., the three-rider ABR set), that reflects that document's vintage, not the current offering; the current suite is the six-condition set (Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, Alzheimer's, Lewy Body). Several primary PDFs are hosted on independent agent/brokerage websites (mirrors of genuine NLG/LSW forms) rather than nationallife.com directly, but form numbers and content match NLG's own materials. State-availability of individual riders (which states exclude Chronic or Critical) could not be confirmed — the specific state-exclusion lists from the legacy brochure were refuted (0-3 and 1-2 votes) and should not be relied upon. The conversion window duration (15 vs 20 years, to age 70) may differ by product and state. One widely-listed source (im_10743.pdf) was confirmed to be an American National document, not NLG/LSW, and was excluded.
openQuestions: What are the current (2025-26) full state-availability exclusion lists for each individual ABR (Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, Alzheimer's) on term — the legacy state-exclusion lists were refuted and no current authoritative list was verified?

Does the conversion window differ by specific product (10-G vs 30-G) and state — is it uniformly 'first 20 years or to age 70 whichever is earlier,' or does the specimen's '15 years' apply to shorter-term products?

What is the exact current marketing form series across all four G-products and ART (the ICC18-20522 series was referenced for 'Level Term xx-G' but per-product form numbers and any state variations were not individually verified)?

What are the minimum face amounts and band/policy-fee structures for US-resident term applicants (as distinct from the $500K foreign-national minimum), and how do Express-class face caps interact with the ART product?
